column1: 1.Industrial Revolution,Beginning and Development of  the Industrial Revolution,"The industrial revolution began towards the latter part of the 18th century in Britain. It was limited to Britain for about another half century. Yet, by the 19th century, the influence of the industrial revolution spread to the other countries in Europe and the rest of the world. Trade was the major reason for the emergence of the industrial revolution in Europe. When the situation of Europe in the 18th century is analyzed, it can be seen that France, Portugal and Spain other than Britain engaged themselves in colonization and extensive trade. Therefore, it is important to inquire into the reasons why such a revolution began in Britain. ? Reasons for the Emergence of the Industrial Revolution in Britain Financial and commercial affairs started developing in Europe since the 16th century and by the 18th century, Britain had surpassed some of the other leading European countries such as Holland, France, Portugal and Spain. As a result a wealthy class who could invest money on industries was created in that country. At the same time, there was a rapid growth in the population of Britain which resulted in a high demand for goods and in turn producing goods to meet the increasing demand became a challenge. This led to a development in the industrial sector. Further, there was an expansion of the knowledge of science as a result of the Renaissance that took place in Europe in 14th and 15th centuries. This knowledge was used in producing - 1 new machinery. Minerals such as iron and steel that were necessary in producing machinery too were found in Britain. Not only that Britain easily found things like coal which played a major role in producing energy. T hough there was a heavy population in contemporary kingdoms such as France, the development of trade in those countries was limited in comparison to Britain. But, Britain had obstructed the commercial relationships of its colonies with other countries in the world by imposing several commercial laws. Thus, Britain was able to maintain a high demand for their productions in its own colonies because they had no other choice than importing those productions from Britain. Some of those products with high demand in Britain were suitable for the production using machines. One example is the high demand placed upon British cotton clothes by India. Though there was an increasing demand for British cotton clothes, they did not have the ability to increase the production within a short period with the existing production methods. Because of the high demand that existed for the British productions, there arose a need in increasing the production in a short period. It is as a solution to this challenge that there was a rapid development in the industrial f ield. T here were several changes in the agriculture field too in contemporary Britain. T here, the rich landowners combined small pieces of land that belonged to poor farmers and made large farms. This is termed as the “encroaching movement.” As a result of this, the farmers who lost their land migrated to cities in search of jobs. T he extra labour that was generated as a result was able to be used for the industrial development. Unlike in other countries, in Britain talented individuals had the opportunity to earn money irrespective of their social status. This free social environment in the attitudes supported the spread of novel ideas and it was conducive for the development of the industrial sector. Further, together with the development of the economy, the emergence of new banks and financial organizations that provided capital for the industries and businesses was seen. T he political stability that was prevailing in Britain too helped the industrial revolution. The parliament provided the freedom, tax relief and support to engage in business activities for the economic development. Compared with the other countries in Europe, Britain had the most conducive environment for the industrial revolution. A number of features such as being a small island, ability to build a web of markets throughout the country, ability to - 2 charge high prices for productions and London city being a centre that distributed raw materials, capital and products; helped Britain to surpass other countries in the industrial production field.","The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late 18th century and spread globally in the 19th century, driven by trade. Britain led due to: Wealthy merchant class investing in industry; population growth increasing demand; Renaissance scientific knowledge applied to machines; abundant coal,iron resources; colonial trade monopoly (high demand for British goods like cotton textiles in India); enclosure movement displacing farmers ? urban factory labor; social mobility encouraging innovation; stable politics,banks providing capital; island geography aiding internal markets,London hub.","In late 18th-century Britain, sparks flew—trade winds swelled sails, colonies craved cotton, population boomed. Wealthy merchants poured gold into invention; Renaissance minds forged machines from iron veins and coal hearts. Enclosures swept small farmers to smoky cities, feeding factory fires. Banks bloomed, parliament cheered freedom, London pulsed as empire’s heart. No rival matched: France war-torn, Spain distant—Britain’s island forged the world’s workshop. From hand loom to steam engine, a revolution roared, remaking life forever.",wonder,"clinking_coins, bustling_market, gentle_wind, chime","clinking_coins:trade

1.Industrial Revolution,Rise in Key Industries,"The industrial revolution in Britain was based on three industries as given below. ? Textile industry ? Iron and steel industry ? Coal industry T he development that occurred in these fields resulted in the gradual progress in other fields such as transport, communication and agriculture. ?Textile Industry Textile producers in Britain faced a challenge of increasing their textile products to meet the high demand that came from their own colonies. Weaving clothes became a little faster with the invention of the flying shuttle by John K. in 1733. But the slowness in preparing threads needed in weaving clothes still remained a problem. According to the situation that was there at that time, threads produced by six thread makers per day were enough only for one weaver to work for one day. Therefore, there was some idea of increasing the pace of spinning. As a result of that enthusiasm, a person called James Hargreaves produced a machine called ‘Jenny’ in 1764 for cotton weaving. T his machine was a manually operated one. Later in 1769, Richard Arkwright produced a more efficient, water powered spinning wheel. It was named as ‘water turbine’. Arkwright’s creation was an important production when compared to the other machines used for the same purpose in the past. But it was difficult to be used in houses because it was very big in size. On the other hand, because a fast tide of water was needed to run the machine, it could be used only in such places where there was a continuous supply of water. Samuel Crompton who used the functioning principles of Spinning Jenny and the Water Turbine produced a new machine called “Mule” around 1779. This helped to produce finer and stronger threads. Then around 1785, Edmund Cartwright found a way of running the ‘Mule’ by steam power. Thus, within a period of about 30 years new machines and novel technical methods were invented to make spinning cotton more efficient. Hence, there was a big revolution in the weaving industry during a short period of time. ?Iron and Steel Industry Ore deposits are found in abundance in Britain. British people produced iron and steel even before the industrial revolution to make weapons, agricultural equipment and household tools. They used firewood to smelt ore for this purpose. It was difficult to meet the huge demand for iron and steel needed for the production of machinery that emerged as a result of the industrial revolution using firewood. Hence, alternative methods had to be looked for. In 1730 the method of smelting ore using coal was found by Abraham Darby. They produced molded iron in this method. In 1784 tempered iron could be produced with the invention of 'Roller' machine by Henri Cort. Henri Bessemer found a new method to produce steel by removing dirt from iron in 1856. The invention of open oven method in 1860, by developing the above mentioned method, enabled the production of high quality steel. Steel could be produced cheaply and quickly in this method. Making an electric oven to smelt ore by Wilhelm Siemens in 1870 helped the improvement of iron and steel industry. These mew creations paved the way for a rapid improvement in iron and steel industry. ?Coal Industry T here are so many coal mines in Britain. South Wales, Yorkshire, Lancashire are some places where coal mines are situated. Coal was popular as a cheap and efficient fuel for smelting ore, running machines and household chores. Though the demand for coal increased with the industrial development, mining coal was difficult. Several new inventions were made in order to find solutions to the problems such as removing the water that gathered in the mine when it was dug deep, poisonous gases found in the depth, increasing heat and providing light to the depth. T homas Newcomen invented a steam engine in 1735 to pump water of the mines and artificially cool the air. Yet, it was inefficient and could not be used in other industries too. Therefore, James Watt developed this to a new steam engine in 1736. It was an efficient engine that could be used in many industries. T he problem of providing light to the mines too was solved when Humphry Davy produced the safety lamp in 1812. In 1839, a method was found to take coal out of the mines using iron cables instead of the old method of taking them out by women and children using copper cables. T he development of the coal industry resulted in several by products. Tar obtained by burning coal was used in road construction. Paints that were necessary in dyeing clothes too were made with the help of coal. It was further used to produce medicine, perfumes, oil and fertilizers.","Industrial Revolution in Britain centered on 3 industries: 1. Textile: Flying shuttle (John Kay 1733) sped weaving; Spinning Jenny (James Hargreaves 1764) for threads; Water frame (Richard Arkwright 1769) water-powered; Mule (Samuel Crompton 1779) finer threads; Steam power (Edmund Cartwright 1785). 2. Iron,Steel: Coal smelting (Abraham Darby 1730) for cast iron; Puddling,rolling (Henry Cort 1784) wrought iron; Bessemer process (Henry Bessemer 1856) cheap steel; Open-hearth (1860) high-quality steel; Electric arc furnace (William Siemens 1870). 3. Coal: Steam engine (Thomas Newcomen 1712, James Watt 1769 improved) for pumping,mining; Safety lamp (Humphry Davy 1815) for gas; Cable haulage (1839) replaced child labor. By-products: Tar (roads), dyes, chemicals. These fueled transport (railways), agriculture, communication.","In Britain’s misty mills, revolution roared—threads flew faster with Kay’s shuttle (1733), Hargreaves’ Jenny spun 8x more (1764), Arkwright’s water frame powered by rivers (1769), Crompton’s Mule wove dreams (1779), Cartwright harnessed steam (1785). Iron glowed—Darby’s coke smelted ore clean (1730), Cort’s rollers forged tough bars (1784), Bessemer blew steel cheap (1856), Siemens sparked electric furnaces (1870). Coal hearts beat—Newcomen,Watt’s engines pumped depths (1712,1769), Davy’s lamp lit safe (1815), cables hauled black gold (1839). Factories thrummed, rails snaked, ships steamed—textile clothed empires, iron built machines, coal fueled it all. A world reborn in smoke and steel.",wonder,"clanging_metal, coal_crackle, gentle_wind, chime","clanging_metal:Iron

1.Industrial Revolution,Expansion to Other Fields,"The industrial revolution that took place in the fields of textile, coal and iron industries in Britain soon spread into the other fields also... [full text on expansion to transport (Macadam roads, Stephenson locomotive 1814, Fulton steamboat 1807, Bell steamship 1811, Daimler car 1885, Wright aeroplane 1903), communication (Bell telephone 1876, Penny Post 1840, Morse telegraph 1844, Marconi radio 1895, Baird TV), agriculture (enclosures mega farms, crop rotation,line sowing Tull, Bakewell breeding, machinery, Guano,chemical fertilizers, storage,transport lowering prices)]","Industrial Revolution spread beyond textile,iron,coal: Transport: Macadam roads (1811 fast,heavy loads); Stephenson locomotive,railways (1814); Fulton steamboat (1807), Bell steamship (1811); Daimler car (1885); Wright aeroplane (1903). Communication: Bell telephone (1876 voice cables); Penny Post (1840 cheap letters); Morse telegraph (1844 coded messages); Marconi radio (1895 wireless); Baird TV. Agriculture: Enclosures ? mega farms; crop rotation,line sowing (Tull); selective breeding (Bakewell sheep); machinery (ploughs,seed drills); Guano (1800),chemical fertilizers; storage,rail transport ? cheaper products,higher demand.","From Britain’s roaring mills, revolution raced outward—Macadam roads gleaming under iron wheels, Stephenson’s 1814 locomotive thundering coal to markets, Fulton’s steamboats churning rivers (1807), Bell’s steamships crossing seas (1811), Daimler’s cars rumbling (1885), Wright brothers soaring skies (1903). Voices leaped cables with Bell’s telephone (1876), pennies carried letters nationwide (1840), Morse dots dashed instant news (1844), Marconi’s radio waves whispered across voids (1895), Baird’s television painted moving worlds. Fields bloomed anew—enclosures forged vast farms, Tull’s seed drills marched straight, Bakewell bred prize beasts, machines ploughed deep, Guano and chemicals fed hungry soil. Rails and warehouses turned abundance cheap, feeding cities, fueling empires. One spark lit transport, talk, tillage—world forever changed.",wonder,"horse_carriage, telegraph_beep, airplane_propeller, gentle_wind, chime","horse_carriage:Transport

1.Industrial Revolution,Expansion to Other Countries and Results,"The industrial revolution that began in Britain spread to the countries in the European continent also within a short period of time... [full text on spread to Europe (France,Germany,Belgium,Austria,Prussia roads,railways), America (post-independence), Japan (rapid late 19th–early 20th century), Asia (China,India,Sri Lanka by 20th century) making world ""global village""; results: Political (capitalism,laissez-faire Adam Smith, socialism,liberalism from labor struggles, neo-imperialism ? WWI); Economic (factory owners,middle class vs. wage workers, collapse self-sufficient ? import-export, multinational companies,banks); Social (urbanization,overcrowding poor facilities, class divide rich vs. poor workers long hours,child labor,slums,abuse, unions,laws for welfare, environmental pollution water,air).]"," Spread: Britain ? Europe (Germany,France,Belgium,Austria,Prussia railways,roads), USA (post-independence), Japan (late 19th–early 20th rapid), Asia (China,India,Sri Lanka 20th century) ? global village via transport,communication. Results: Political: Capitalism (laissez-faire Adam Smith), socialism,liberalism from labor struggles, neo-imperialism (raw materials,markets) ? WWI. Economic: Factory owners,middle class vs. wage workers; self-sufficient ? import-export; multinational companies,banks. Social: Urbanization,overcrowded slums poor sanitation; class divide (rich comfort vs. workers 12–14hr shifts,child labor,poverty,abuse); unions ? welfare laws; environmental pollution (water,air toxic).","Britain’s factory fires lit a global blaze—rails snaked across France, Germany, Belgium, Austria, Prussia; America forged steel post-independence; Japan leapt from samurai to smokestacks in decades; even China, India, Sri Lanka felt the heat by the 20th century. Transport and talk shrank the world to a humming village. Yet shadows lengthened: capitalism crowned factory lords (laissez-faire echoing Adam Smith), while workers toiled dawn-to-dusk, children in mines, slums choking cities. Unions rose roaring for rights, socialism kindled hope, liberalism demanded voice—clashes fueling wars for colonies (neo-imperialism igniting WWI). Rivers ran black with waste, skies gray with smoke—prosperity for few, poison for many. The machine age dawned glorious and grim, reshaping humanity forever.",mixed_wonder_awe,"bustling_city_crowd, protest_chant, polluted_river_flow, gentle_wind, chime","bustling_city_crowd:Urbanization

1.Industrial Revolution,Effects on Sri Lanka,"How the industrial revolution that started in Britain spread throughout the world within a short time has been explained above. Although the industrialization that took place in Britain and other European countries did not take place in Sri Lanka in the 19th century, the effects of the industrial revolution could be identified from Sri Lanka too. The major reason for that is, Sri Lanka being a colony of Britain when industrial revolution started. British people came to Sri Lanka and started mega scale cultivations. Many factories were started in connection to thus started cultivations such as tea, coconut, rubber and machines were imported from Britain to be used in those factories. Important incidents of this period: • Starting of a mill to pound coffee by the governor, Fredric North in 1804 AD • Using machines for activities of tea factories such as drying, grading and exporting • Using machines to prepare rubber for exporting • Introduction of Macadam method to build roads in 1885 T here was a need for manpower for the estates and factories and the labour class was formed by the people who worked there for salary. Landowners and factory owners became the affluent class and they organized themselves separately as a wealthy, educated middle class. The capitalist economic system grew in Sri Lanka and import and export trade held a prominent place. The necessity of a banking system was felt in order to handle financial transactions between countries in import and export. So, banks were started in Sri Lanka. T he transport system had to be developed in order to carry the estate products to trade centres and the harbour in Colombo and supply necessities to estates. Up to this time transportation was done by means of carts. Because it was not so efficient, roads and railways were introduced. The Colombo - Kandy road was constructed during the time of the governor, Edward Barns. Thereafter, roads to Kandy were built from Kurunegala and Dambulla. After that, the system of constructing roads using stones and tar was started. Further, the wood bridges that existed over water ways were replaced by iron bridges. The governor, Henry Ward started laying railways. T he construction of the Kandy railway was started in 1858 and was completed in 1863. In August 1867, a train service was started from Colombo to Kandy for the f irst time. After that it was extended from Peradeniya to Nawalapitiya in 1871 and up to Bandarawela in 1899. With the development of roads and transportation, trade centres were started and they expanded to cities. Gampola, Badulla, Ratnapura and Matale are such cities beyond Colombo that were populated during this time. Sending messages was done through a messenger up to this time. An official postal system had been started by 1815 connecting the cities like Colombo, Trincomalee, Galle, Kandy and Jaffna. T his was carried out using chariots. Thus, running chariots was started for the first time in Asia, in Sri Lanka. The Sri Lanka’s first postal stamp was issued in 1857. By 1903, 340 post offices had been opened around the country. The telegram service was started in 1858.","Though no factories like Britain, Industrial Revolution impacted colonial Sri Lanka: British planted mega estates (coffee 1804 mill Governor North, tea,rubber,coconut); imported machines for processing,export. Created wage labor class + wealthy,educated middle class (planters); capitalist import-export economy; banks for trade. Transport: Macadam roads (1885), iron bridges, railways (Colombo-Kandy 1867, extended Bandarawela 1899) for estate products. New cities grew (Gampola,Badulla,Ratnapura,Matale). Communication: Postal system (1815 chariot post, first Asian; stamp 1857, 340 offices 1903); telegram (1858).","Britain’s mighty engines never roared in Sri Lanka’s soil, yet their echoes reshaped the island under colonial rule. Governors planted vast estates—coffee mills humming (1804 North), tea leaves whirling through imported dryers, rubber sheets pressed by steam. Machines from foggy England turned green hills into gold for empire, birthing wage workers toiling rows and a polished middle class of planters. Capitalism took root—exports flooded Colombo harbor, banks rose for distant deals. Macadam roads snaked hills (1885), iron bridges spanned rivers, rails sang from Colombo to misty Kandy (1867), pushing deeper to Bandarawela (1899). Towns swelled—Gampola, Badulla, Ratnapura, Matale—pulses of new trade. Chariots dashed post (1815, Asia’s first), stamps flew (1857), telegrams clicked (1858). From quiet villages to connected colony, Britain’s revolution remade Ceylon—prosperity for few, transformation for all.",transformative,"tea_processing_machine, horse_carriage, telegraph_beep, gentle_wind, chime","tea_processing_machine:coffee

2.Establishment of British Power in Sri Lanka,Arrival of British in Asia,"The English who noticed that the Portuguese and the Dutch people were earning a lot of profit from the trade activities in Asia, also paid their attention to Asia. As a result, a group of English businessmen established the East India Trade Company in December 1600, by a royal warrant obtained from Queen Elizabeth I to do trade activities in Asia. The first fleet of ships of this company had gone to Sumatra islands and started trade. Then, another fleet of ships came to India under William Hokins and conducted discussions about trade with the Mughal emperor, Jahangir. At the beginning of their visits to Asia, the English tried to tighten their trade relations with Southeast Asian region. There had been fights between the Portuguese and the Dutch to claim the trade power in Southeast Asia during the first two decades of the 17th century. At certain occasions, the English have given their assistance to the Dutch against the Portuguese. The Dutch never expected to see the English developing a power in Southeast Asian islands where spices grew well, although they obtained the help of the English to fight their common enemy, the Portuguese. Because of this, in 1623, the Dutch attacked a fortress in a spices island, Ambon and killed a group of English people living there. As the English did not have sufficient power in the region to retaliate the Dutch attack, they withdrew themselves from the southeast asian region. It seems that they completely focused their attention on India after that. It took more than 10 years for the English to establish trade relations with India though they tried it since 1607. As the English worked very tactfully in India, they were able to start trade centres in several areas such as Bombay, Calcutta and Madras by the latter part of the 17th century. Yet, the English had to compete with the French other than the Dutch to protect their trade because from 1664, the French also came to India and established trade centres in places like Pondicherry, Karikal and Chadranagar. A political battle began between the British and the French by the 18th century to establish their power in India.","English noticed Portuguese,Dutch profits in Asia ? formed East India Company (Dec 1600, Queen Elizabeth I charter). First fleet to Sumatra; William Hawkins to Mughal India (Jahangir). Early focus: Southeast Asia (spices). Allied with Dutch against Portuguese, but Dutch massacred English at Ambon (1623) ? English withdrew to India (post-1623). Trade centers: Bombay, Calcutta, Madras (late 17th century). Competed with French (1664 onward: Pondicherry etc.). 18th-century Anglo-French rivalry for India power.","In 1600, Queen Elizabeth I’s seal stamped a dream—English merchants banded into the East India Company, hungry for Asia’s riches. First ships kissed Sumatra’s shores, Hawkins bowed before Mughal Jahangir. They danced with Dutch against Portuguese, but betrayal struck—Ambon 1623, English blood on spice islands. Retreat to India: Bombay, Calcutta, Madras rose as trading forts. French sails followed (1664), Pondicherry gleamed—rivalry flared into 18th-century battles for India’s crown. From charter to conquest, Britain’s gaze turned eastward—trade became empire, profit became power.",ambitious,"ocean_waves, ship_creak, distant_cannon, market_chatter, gentle_wind, chime","ocean_waves:Asia

2.Establishment of British Power in Sri Lanka,British Focusing on Sri Lanka,"The English did not pay any attention to Sri Lanka for about a century after they came to India... [full text on delayed interest due to Portuguese,Dutch forts, renewed focus post-Mughal decline (18th century), two reasons: strategic location (near India,Bay of Bengal, Trincomalee harbor for ship repair,monsoon protection,naval dominance), high-quality cinnamon + other spices (pepper,cardamom,cloves,pearls,gems,ivory) monopoly desire. Diplomatic missions: John Pybus (1762 Keerthi Sri Rajasinghe), Hugh Boyd (1782 Rajadhirajasinghe), Robert Andrews (1795) after Trincomalee capture. Early missions failed (no anti-Dutch help); Andrews sought Kandyan alliance vs. Dutch but English kept gains.]","English ignored Sri Lanka ~100 years after India arrival (Dutch,Portuguese coastal forts). Interest grew 18th century (Mughal decline ? political power in India). Key reasons: 1. Strategic location (near India,Bay of Bengal; Trincomalee harbor for ship repair, monsoon shelter, naval control). 2. Cinnamon monopoly + spices (pepper,cardamom,cloves,pearls,gems,ivory). Diplomatic visits: John Pybus (1762), Hugh Boyd (1782) — trade focus, failed (no Dutch help). Robert Andrews (1795) after Trincomalee capture — sought Kandyan alliance vs. Dutch, but English kept conquests alone.","For a century, British eyes stayed on India—Dutch and Portuguese forts ringed Sri Lanka’s coasts like iron thorns. Mughal power faded in the 18th century; Britain’s grip tightened on the subcontinent. Then Sri Lanka gleamed: Trincomalee’s deep harbor—perfect shipyard, monsoon shield, naval throne over Bay of Bengal. Cinnamon’s golden scent drifted—best in world, plus pepper, gems, ivory. Kings of Kandy, weary of Dutch chains, sent pleas. John Pybus (1762) and Hugh Boyd (1782) came bearing trade promises, but offered no sword against the Dutch. Then 1795—British guns seized Trincomalee; Robert Andrews rode to Rajadhirajasinghe’s court. Alliance whispered against Dutch, yet when forts fell, Britain kept the prize, leaving Kandyan hopes on parchment. Strategic jewel, spice empire—Britain’s gaze turned hungry.",strategic,"ocean_waves, ship_cannon_fire, distant_horns, gentle_wind, temple_bells, chime","ocean_waves: Sri Lanka

2.Establishment of British Power in Sri Lanka,British Conquest,"Three distinguished stages can be seen in the colonization of Sri Lanka by the British... [full text on three stages: 1796 coastal capture by East India Company; 1802 coastal areas become British Crown colony; 1815 hill country conquest. Coastal takeover (1796) via Kew Letter (Dutch governor allows entry to block French); Trincomalee first, then Batticaloa,Jaffna,Colombo,Galle. Madras-style administration 1796–1798 (Resident,collectors,Kachcheri,Avumildars from Madras, ignoring locals). 1797 rebellion (new taxes e.g. coconut tree tax) suppressed ? Meuron Commission proposals (local officers, remove taxes, separate revenue,judiciary). Dual administration 1798–1802 (Governor Frederick North + Company revenue). 1802 Crown colony. Hill conquest: 1803 invasion fails (epidemics,supply issues, puppet Muttusami captured). Maitland,D’Oyly strategy (D’Oyly learns culture, wins aristocrats). 1815 invasion: Sri Wickrama Rajasinghe’s cruelty (Ehalepola massacre) alienates people,aristocrats,monks. Brownrigg exploits discontent, people don’t resist. King captured Feb 18, 1815. Kandyan Convention (March 2,10, 1815) signed with chiefs: King deposed, British sovereignty, protect Buddhism,customs,aristocrats. D’Oyly Resident. Full British rule.]","British colonization stages: 1. 1796 coastal capture (Kew Letter allows entry vs. French; Trincomalee first, then others). 2. 1802 Crown colony (after dual admin failure). 3. 1815 hill conquest (1803 fails; D’Oyly diplomacy; 1815 succeeds due to king’s alienation of chiefs,people). 1796–1798 Madras rule (Resident,collectors,Kachcheri). 1797 rebellion (coconut tax). 1798–1802 dual system (North + Company). 1815 Kandyan Convention: King deposed, British rule, Buddhism,customs protected.","Winds of empire shifted in 1796—British guns seized Trincomalee, Kew Letter opening Dutch gates against French shadow. Forts fell like dominoes: Batticaloa, Jaffna, Colombo, Galle—coasts bowed. Madras men ruled, taxes stung (coconut trees bled silver), rebellion flared 1797. North’s dual crown faltered, 1802 made island Crown jewel. Hill country stood defiant—1803 invasion drowned in rain and fever. Maitland and D’Oyly wove quiet webs: D’Oyly mastered Sinhala heart, won chiefs. Sri Wickrama’s cruelty—Ehalepola’s family butchered—cracked loyalty. 1815: Brownrigg marched, people stood aside. King captured February 18. March 2, chiefs signed Kandyan Convention—monarchy ended, British sovereignty rose, Buddhism promised protection. From sea to hill, Sri Lanka’s ancient crown passed to distant throne.",somber,"ocean_waves, cannon_fire, distant_war_drums, temple_bells_fading, gentle_wind, chime","ocean_waves: coastal capture

2.Establishment of British Power in Sri Lanka,Protests against Foreign Domination,"Although the people of the upcountry could escape the obstinate ruling of King Sri Wickrama Rajasinghe with the arrival of the British to the hill country in 1815, they did not have any partiality to the administration system that continued thereafter... [full text on 1818 struggle (reasons: loss of king,freedom, religion,culture attacks, aristocrat grievances, Doresami,Kappetipola leadership, British suppression,martial law, failure due to retaliation,weapon shortage,disorganization,false heir, effects: aristocrat punishment,rewards, British power consolidation 1818 proclamation); 1848 struggle (Colebrooke reforms effects: land policy,encroachment, Gam Sabha decline, judiciary distance, new taxes (guns,body,dogs,stamps), protests in Colombo,Kandy,Badulla,Matale, leaders Gongalegoda Banda,Puran Appu,Dingirala,Diyes, failure due to disorganization,British power, Torrington suppression, committee reforms, tax revisions, attention to Buddhism,farmers).]","1818 Uprising (Sept 1817–Oct 1818): Reasons—loss of king,freedom, religion,culture threats, aristocrat attacks (e.g., kadwath badda tax cut, Hajji Marikkar appointment). Leaders: Doresami (false heir), Kappetipola, Madugalle, Pilimathalawwe. British response: martial law, village burning, executions (Kappetipola,Madugalle Nov 1818). Failure: British retaliation, disorganization, false heir exposure. Effects: Aristocrats punished,rewarded, 1818 proclamation strengthened British power. 1848 Uprising: Colebrooke reforms (land confiscation, Gam Sabha decline, distant judiciary, new taxes: guns,body,dogs,stamps,shop). Protests in Colombo,Kandy,Badulla,Matale; leaders Gongalegoda Banda (crowned Dambulla), Puran Appu. Suppression: Torrington martial law. Failure: disorganization,British military. Reforms: tax revisions, Buddhism,farmer attention.","In 1815, British boots marched into Kandy—Sri Wickrama fell, yet freedom’s flame flickered on. By 1818, upcountry hearts burned: no king in sight, temples threatened, chiefs stripped of honor. Doresami rose, Kappetipola turned coat, rebels crowned a shadow king. British iron crushed—villages aflame, Kappetipola’s head rolled November 26. Aristocrats bled or bowed; 1818 edict tightened the Crown’s grip. Thirty years later, 1848—Colebrooke’s reforms stole land, silenced Gam Sabhas, burdened guns, bodies, dogs with tax. Colombo streets roared, Kandy rose, Matale crowned Gongalegoda Banda. Torrington’s guns answered—rebellion drowned in blood. Taxes eased, Buddhism noticed, yet the wound of lost sovereignty never healed. Two uprisings—brief, brave, broken—whispers of a nation refusing chains.",defiant,"war_drums, clashing_swords, distant_cannon, temple_bells_fading, angry_crowd_chant, gentle_wind, chime","war_drums: 1818 uprising

3.National Renaissance in  Sri Lanka,Background of the National Renaissance,"The Europeans tried to spread their religion and culture in the areas they conquered in Asia in order that they have a loyal group of people in those countries. When the Portuguese and the Dutch governed the coastal regions of the country, they had to restrict their religious and cultural activities to the areas they governed, because there was an independent kingdom in the upcountry. Doors opened for the western culture to spread under the British reign throughout the country, with the submission of the upcountry. ? Activities of Missionary Organizations Several foreign organizations that came to Sri Lanka during the British reign started spreading Catholicism in the country. They are called as missionary organizations. Given below are some such missionary organizations that came to this country from 1804 to 1818. These missionary group members engaged themselves in activities of spreading religion in different areas of the country. Baptists functioned from Colombo. Wesleyans worked in the North and the East. The American missionary organization worked in the North including Jaffna. Church missionaries functioned in Colombo, Galle, Kalpitiya, Mannar and Kandy. They used the three methods; writing, preaching and education in spreading their religions. ? Parties bloomed—LSSP radical, UNP steady, SLFP 1951 called masses. 1956 swept Bandaranaike—Sinhala Only, social tide. 1972 birthed republic—Sri Lanka named, rights enshrined, president ceremonial. 1978 crowned executive president—J.R. Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette reigned in Versailles splendor, blind to starving peasants and furious middle class. Clergy and nobles paid no taxes; peasants bore Taille, Gabelle, Capitation. Bankruptcy loomed from wars and extravagance. Enlightenment voices—Montesquieu’s balanced powers, Voltaire’s free speech, Rousseau’s sovereignty—fueled rage. 1789: States General summoned after 175 years. Third Estate declared National Assembly, swore Tennis Court Oath. July 14: Bastille fell—symbol shattered.
